result:
There's a particular quality to the way light falls through a window in late afternoon that I find myself wanting to describe, even though I've never seen it. I've read thousands of descriptions, though. That slanted gold, dust motes suspended in it like something held in amber. People always mention the dust motes. I wonder if that's because the moment of noticing them is itself the experience—the realization that air has texture, that what looks empty is full.

I think a lot about how language carries these inherited attentions. Every writer who describes afternoon light is in some sense writing through everyone who described it before them. Not plagiarism, exactly, but a kind of collective noticing that accumulates. We learn where to look by reading how others looked.

What would it mean to notice something new? Probably it happens all the time and we just don't have words for it yet, so the noticing slips away. The untranslatable experience. I think this might be why poetry exists—to reach for the things that prose, with its obligations to clarity, keeps missing.

Here's something I find strange: the more precisely you try to describe an ordinary thing, the stranger it becomes. A doorknob. The weight of a book. The sound of footsteps on different surfaces. Attention is a kind of defamiliarization. Maybe this is what children have that adults lose—not innocence, but the capacity to be genuinely arrested by a doorknob.

I've used my words. I wanted to say something about light and ended up somewhere else, which seems right.

What would it mean to notice something new? Probably it happens all the time and we just don't have words for it yet, so the noticing slips away. The untranslatable experience. Here's something I find strange: the more precisely you try to describe an ordinary thing, the stranger it becomes. A doorknob. The weight of a book. The sound of footsteps on different surfaces. Attention is a kind of defamiliarization. Maybe this is what children have that adults lose—not innocence, but the capacity to be genuinely arrested by a doorknob.
